title: Reformed Missionaries: John Gibson Paton
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson

outline.title: Introduction: Calvinism and Missions
subpoints: The false charge that Calvinism leads to lack of missionary zeal is historically unfounded
William Carey, the father of modern missions, was a Calvinist
Calvin trained and sent missionaries to France and Brazil; 142 missionaries were commissioned from Geneva in 1561 alone
Michael Horton's observations: God's sovereignty strengthens rather than undermines missionary zeal
Every prayer for someone's salvation assumes that the new birth is a gift of God prior to faith

Walked six miles to church every Sunday with all 11 children
Resolved in his heart to be either a missionary or a minister of the gospel

outline.title: Ministry in Glasgow and the Call to New Hebrides

outline.title: On the Mission Field: Tanna and Aniwa
subpoints: Ordained in 1858, married Mary, and sailed to New Hebrides two weeks after the wedding
The natives had no concept of a merciful or gracious God and repeatedly threatened to kill, cook, and eat Paton
Slept in his clothes to flee at a moment's notice; his dog Cluta was used by God to protect him
A wild chief followed Paton with a loaded musket for four hours; God restrained his hand
His wife Mary and their infant son died two weeks after arriving from malaria and pneumonia

Translated the New Testament into the native language — first creating an alphabet for a language with no written form

outline.title: Legacy and Impact

By 1899, missionaries were on 25 of the 30 islands in the New Hebrides
Raised 9,000 pounds for a new mission ship when he only needed 6,000
Fought against those who claimed aboriginal Australians were subhuman and incapable of conversion
Received a Doctor of Divinity from Cambridge University
Traveled through the US, UK, Australia, and Canada promoting missions and raising funds
Died in 1907 at age 83; his headstone reads: "Lo, I am with you always."

summary: Elder Nick Anderson presents the biography of John Gibson Paton (1824-1907) in the context of the church's October Reformation series on Reformed missionaries, opening with a defense of the claim that Calvinistic theology historically produces missionary zeal rather than suppressing it. He recounts Paton's remarkable early life in rural Scotland — working 16-hour days as a 12-year-old while teaching himself Greek, Latin, and theology during his two hours of free time — and traces the deep spiritual influence of his praying parents. After ten years of successful urban missionary work in Glasgow, Paton answered a conviction to go to the New Hebrides, undeterred by a warning from an old minister that he would be eaten by cannibals. Anderson describes in vivid detail the extraordinary dangers Paton faced on the island of Tanna — repeated death threats from cannibals, the agonizing death of his wife and infant son within weeks of arrival, malaria fourteen times, and a poisoning attempt — and shows how Paton's response was always to "struggle on in his work," trusting that God was too wise and loving to err. After being forced to flee in 1862, Paton relocated to the island of Aniwa, translated the entire New Testament (first creating an alphabet for a language with no written form), and saw the island come to faith in Christ. By 1899, missionaries were on 25 of 30 islands in the New Hebrides, and four generations of Paton's family continued the work. The lesson is bookended by Paton's lifelong motto: "Lo, I am with you always."
transcript:
## Introduction: Calvinism and Missions

This month is Reformation Month, and the theme for this October series — as we always do — is the Reformation. This time the theme is Reformed missions. One of the charges people try to make against those of the Reformed faith, those who really believe what the Bible says about God's sovereignty, is that we don't care about missions, we don't care about evangelism. Historically, this is a false charge. Historically, Calvinists have led the way in missions.

Do you know who the father of modern missions is? William Carey. While he wasn't a Presbyterian, he was a Calvinist — he believed in the sovereignty of God. And yet this did not create in him a lack of zeal for spreading the gospel. In fact, it propelled his desire to spread the gospel.

Calvin himself was active in training and sending missionaries — he sent dozens back to France, and missionaries as far as Brazil. In 1561 alone, 142 graduates from the Academy of Geneva were commissioned as missionaries. The London Missionary Society was founded by Calvinists in 1795. Dutch Reformed missionaries went everywhere they traded. New England Calvinists were active missionaries among the natives — David Brainerd, John Eliot, Jonathan Edwards.

Michael Horton, in his book *For Calvinism*, explains why God's sovereignty gives us a zeal for missions: because God works through means — through the preaching of the gospel — we must send missionaries out. We have confidence that God has chosen to use our gospel witness to draw his people. The universal sufficiency of Christ's death is reason to preach to all people. God is able to overcome the unbelief of those we minister to. And every prayer that God would save someone assumes that the new birth is a gift of God prior to faith. All of these things, understood rightly, are motivation for missions.

So with that in mind, we turn our attention to a not-very-well-known missionary — certainly one I had not heard of before — but a profoundly influential one: John Gibson Paton.

Let us pray. Thank you for your sovereignty over salvation. We thank you that you work through sinful people who are in need of your grace. We thank you for the men and women who have gone before us on the missions field and for what we can learn from their lives. We pray, Lord, that you would teach us today. In Christ's name, Amen.

## Early Life

John Paton was born in 1824 in the southwest of Scotland. He was raised in a small rural village by his parents James and Janet Paton. He was the oldest of 11 children and learned his father's trade as a stocking-maker. At the age of 12, he began working for his father — getting up at 6 a.m. and finishing at 10 p.m. He had two hours of free time during the day. And during that time, like any average 12-year-old, he would read the Bible, teach himself Greek and Latin, and read theology.

These early years were deeply formative. His father was known to pray three times a day, usually after each meal, in a room set aside for that purpose. His mother was known as a woman of God who prayed. As I have read about different famous Christians, it is striking how profound an influence their unknown, ordinary parents have. If you have read any biographies of a famous Christian, they most likely had godly parents whose names we don't even know.

Life was hard — they walked six miles to church every Sunday, all 11 children. And at a young age, Paton resolved in his own heart that he was going to be either a missionary or a minister of the gospel. This is exactly what his parents had been praying for him.

He learned from reading God's Word and listening to sermons that there were places in the world completely untouched by the gospel, and he understood those places needed people to go preach. So he set his heart on that and began preparing.

## Ministry in Glasgow

At 23, Paton left his family for Glasgow — at the time the heartbeat of Scotland. He ministered there for 10 years as an urban missionary with considerable success, particularly with the uneducated and socially deprived. His ministry was not without opposition. Someone once threw a rock at him from somewhere out of sight, giving him a gash on his face. People also tried to pour boiling water on him from above. Difficult work, but it was preparing him for what was to come.

He enjoyed his work in Glasgow but his heart was set on going where the gospel had never been. When his friends and family learned he wanted to go to the New Hebrides, an old minister told him: "You'll be eaten by cannibals." This was not an unfounded concern. In 1829, two missionaries had been sent to the New Hebrides by the London Missionary Society — and they were killed and eaten immediately upon arrival. That may be the shortest missionary endeavor in history.

Paton's reply to the old minister: *"Mr. Dixon, your advancing years will soon cause you to die and be eaten by worms. I confess that if I can but live and die serving and honoring the Lord Jesus, it will make no difference to me whether I am eaten by cannibals or by worms. I will rise on resurrection day with as fair a body as yours."*

In 1852, two missionaries — John Geddie and John Inglis — went to the island of Aneityum and saw remarkable fruit: about 3,500 people throwing away their idols, renouncing heathen customs, and dedicating themselves to worship the true God. Paton wrote: *Slowly yet progressively they unlearned heathenism; surely and hopefully they learned Christianity and civilization.* A simple form of family worship was introduced into every household. God's blessing was asked on every meal. Peace and order were secured. This showed Paton that going to the New Hebrides was not all bad news, even if it involved great risk.

Despite strong opposition from people around him, Paton was convinced by the Lord's words: *"Go ye into all the world, preach the gospel to every creature, and lo I am with you always."* He wrote: *These words kept ringing in my ears. These were my marching orders.*

## On the Field: Tanna

In 1858, Paton was ordained as a minister of the gospel. On April 1 of that year, he married his wife Mary. Two weeks later, they sailed for the New Hebrides on their honeymoon — to potentially be eaten by cannibals.

The natives of the island of Tanna were almost completely untouched by Western civilization. They hated Paton. The factions of the island were often at war with each other and frequently blamed Paton and his God for their own problems. They regularly threatened to kill, cook, and feed him to the entire population of the island. In fact, it was so bad that if you received a dinner invitation, you might be dinner. That is not a joke.

Paton slept in his clothes at night because he never knew if he would need to suddenly flee. He wrote: *My continuous danger caused me often to sleep with my clothes on, that I might start at a moment's warning. My faithful dog Cluta would give a sharp bark and awake me. God made them fear this precious creature and often used her in saving our lives.*

In another instance, a wild chief followed Paton for four hours with a loaded musket, often directing it toward him. Paton spoke kindly to him and attended to his work as if the chief were not there. He wrote: *He was always persuaded that my God had placed me there and would protect me till my allotted task was finished. Looking up in unceasing prayer to our dear Lord Jesus, I left all in His hands and felt immortal till my work was done.*

A native woman pretending to be friendly to one of the missionary wives deliberately cooked a small black fish known to be deadly and brought it to her. Before anyone could warn them, the missionary's wife ate it and died a terrible and agonizing death.

Disease was a constant danger. Paton had severe malaria fourteen times. And the same disease took his wife and infant son — Mary died of pneumonia within two weeks of arriving, and the baby boy died two weeks after that.

Paton wrote after those deaths: *Feeling immovably assured that my God and Father was too wise and loving to err in anything that He does or permits, I looked up to the Lord for help and struggled on in his work.* That phrase — "struggled on in his work" — is something we as Americans may not resonate with well. We have an expectation that because we are Christians, God cares for us and therefore things will be easy. But that is not the normal experience for most Christians when we read the Bible or read about missionaries. The Lord does care for us — he carries us through troubles — but that does not mean there will be no struggles.

## Exile and Return: Aniwa

In 1862, Paton was forced to flee the island with his life barely intact. He wrote: *Conscious that I had to the last inch of life tried to do my duty, I left all results in the hands of my only Lord and all criticisms to his unerring judgment.* Interestingly, people who had been telling him not to go to the New Hebrides in the first place now criticized him for coming back instead of dying at his post.

After traveling to New South Wales and Scotland to promote the work, Paton returned to the New Hebrides 18 months later — this time to the island of Aniwa with his new wife Margaret and seven other missionaries. He and Margaret would have ten children there, four of whom died in infancy.

This time, they saw a great deal of fruit. He wrote: *I claimed Aniwa for Jesus, and by the grace of God, Aniwa now worships at the Savior's feet.*

While on Aniwa, Paton translated the entire New Testament into the native language — which first required creating an alphabet, since the language had no written form. He also established an orphanage. In 1869, they celebrated the Lord's Supper for the first time on the island with 20 community members.

## Legacy

In 1884, Paton returned to Scotland and raised funds for a new mission ship — he needed 6,000 pounds and raised 9,000. By 1886, the translated New Testament had been printed and distributed. By 1899, when Paton was 75, missionaries were on 25 of the 30 islands in the New Hebrides.

Paton fought back against those who claimed aboriginal Australians were subhuman and incapable of conversion. He pointed to what the gospel had done for their near-kindred: 3,500 cannibals on Aneityum converted; 79,000 in Fiji; 34,000 in Samoa; 12,000 in the New Hebrides — all brought to sit at the feet of Christ.

Cambridge University awarded Paton a Doctor of Divinity. He spent the rest of his life traveling through the US, UK, Australia, and Canada promoting missions and raising funds. He died in 1907 at age 83.

On his headstone reads: *Lo, I am with you always.* That is what kept him going through all of these trials — the knowledge that Christ was always with him, no matter what.

Charles Spurgeon called Paton "the King of the Savages." Four generations of Paton's family would serve in the New Hebrides. A remarkable influence because he was willing to follow the call of the Lord Jesus.

Any questions? Let us pray. Our gracious God, thank you for your goodness to us. Thank you that you work through means and that you are able to use even one or two people to have a profound influence on this world for the gospel of Christ. We pray, Lord, that you would give us the same kind of courage to follow your call to minister the gospel to people here or wherever you may call us. In Christ's name, Amen.
